## Canada Health Act Annual Report 2024-2025
Download in PDF format (54.54 MB, 407 pages)
Organization: Health Canada
Date published: 2026-02-13
Cat.: H1-4E-PDF ISBN: 1497-9144 Pub.: 250430
- Canada Health Act 2024-2025 year in review infographic
- Chapter 1 – Canada Health Act overview
- Chapter 2 – Administration and compliance
- Chapter 3 – Provincial and territorial health care insurance plans in 2024-2025 Insurance Plan Descriptions Newfoundland and Labrador Prince Edward Island Nova Scotia New Brunswick Quebec Ontario Manitoba Saskatchewan Alberta British Columbia Yukon Northwest Territories Nunavut
- Insurance Plan Descriptions
- Newfoundland and Labrador
- Prince Edward Island
- Northwest Territories
- Annex A – Canada Health Act and Extra-Billing and User Charges Information Regulations
- Annex B – Policy interpretation letters
- Annex C – Financial statements of actual amounts of extra-billing and user charges for the period April 1, 2022 to March 31, 2023
- Annex D – Reimbursement action plans and progress reports
Health Canada would like to acknowledge the work and effort that went into producing this Annual Report. It is through the dedication and timely commitment of the following departments of health and their staff that we are able to bring you this report on the administration and operation of the Canada Health Act:
- Newfoundland and Labrador Department of Health and Community Services
- Prince Edward Island Department of Health and Wellness
- Nova Scotia Department of Health and Wellness
- New Brunswick Department of Health
- Quebec Ministry of Health and Social Services
- Ontario Ministry of Health
- Manitoba Health, Seniors, and Long-Term Care
- Saskatchewan Ministry of Health
- Alberta Ministry of Health
- British Columbia Ministry of Health
- Yukon Department of Health and Social Services
- Northwest Territories Department of Health and Social Services
- Nunavut Department of Health
We also greatly appreciate the extensive work effort that was put into this report by our production team, including desktop publishers, translators, editors and concordance experts, printers and staff of Health Canada.
It is an honour to serve Canadians as the federal Minister of Health during a time when access to timely, high-quality health care is top of mind, and to present to Parliament and to Canadians the 2024–2025 Canada Health Act Annual Report.
This report highlights the collective work of federal, provincial, and territorial governments in upholding the principles of the Canada Health Act, ensuring that access to medically necessary care is based on medical need—not the ability or willingness to pay. This work also aligns with Canada's economic priorities, recognizing that a strong and accessible health care system supports a resilient economy by helping Canadians stay healthy, productive, and able to participate fully in the workforce.
At the Health Ministers' Meeting in Calgary, we reaffirmed our shared commitment, across all levels of government, to immediate and ongoing action to deliver better health care for Canadians. We recognize that our health care system is under significant strain, as are the frontline health workers who deliver care every day—leaving too many Canadians facing barriers to accessing the care they need.
These challenges are not new. Prior to the introduction of the Canada Health Act, there were significant concerns that Medicare was being eroded, the system was under great pressure, and access to care was threatened by increasing charges to patients:
"It is apparent that unless a concerted effort is made to commit ourselves once more to the principles of universal health insurance, the gaps in insurance coverage will continue to grow. A choice must now be made. Do we as a nation wish to allow an increasing number of our fellow Canadians to fall through the widening cracks in our health insurance program? Or do we wish instead to preserve and improve Medicare?"
Excerpted from "Preserving Universal Medicare – A Government of Canada Position Paper." (1983)
The past response to these challenges was a commitment to universal health care with the unanimous passing of the Canada Health Act in the House of Commons. As Minister of Health, I take my role as guardian of the Canada Health Act seriously. Canadians have long believed that everyone should be able to access medically necessary care on the same fair terms, regardless of their ability to pay. This belief remains the foundation of our publicly funded health system and is why improving access to care is one of my top priorities.
Since the Canada Health Act was passed in 1984, the delivery of health care has evolved, and the interpretation of the Canada Health Act has also evolved, to keep pace. I commend jurisdictions for their innovative work in expanding the scopes of practice of regulated health professionals to allow practitioners to meet the growing needs of Canadians. However, this innovation should benefit all – and we must continue our work to ensure that Canadians are not asked to pay for services already funded through their taxes. The Canada Health Act Services Policy clarifies that medically necessary services—whether provided by a physician or another regulated health professional, such as a nurse practitioner, with the scope of practice to provide them—are expected to be covered under provincial and territorial health insurance plans. While the policy takes effect April 1, 2026, provinces and territories would first report any patient charges for physician-equivalent services, should they occur, beginning in December 2028.
We will also continue to collaborate and advance initiatives under the Working Together to Improve Health Care for Canadians plan, backed by nearly $200 billion in federal investments over ten years. This includes $25 billion to help advance shared health priorities with provinces and territories, including efforts to expand access to family health services, strengthen health workforce capacity, improve mental health and substance use care, and modernize health systems so they work better for patients and providers alike.
At a time when national unity matters more than ever—in health care and across every aspect of our shared future—we will continue strengthening our partnerships with provinces and territories to ensure we are always working toward what truly matters: the health and well-being of all Canadians.

## Canada Health Act 2024-2025 year in review infographic
- On January 9, 2025, the new Canada Health Act Services Policy was announced and confirms that patients should not be charged for medically necessary services when provided by regulated health professionals, such as nurse practitioners, pharmacists, or midwives, if those services would be covered if provided by a physician.
- The policy takes effect April 1, 2026, and provinces and territories will first report any patient charges for these services, should they occur, beginning in December 2028.
- In 2024-2025, the federal government transferred over $52.1 billion to provinces and territories through the Canada Health Transfer, to support them in delivering publicly funded care.
- In 2024-2025, approximately $1.04 billion was reciprocally paid by provinces and territories, excluding QC, for insured hospital and physician services provided to residents outside their home jurisdiction, through reciprocal billing agreements in place between the provinces and territories.
- In March 2025, more than $62.2 million in Canada Health Transfer deductions were levied to NL, NS, NB, QC, ON, SK, AB, BC, and YK as a result of patient charges for medically necessary services.
- Based on actions taken to ensure patients do not face charges for medically necessary services, in March 2025, more than $51.9 million was reimbursed to NL, NS, NB, QC, ON, AB, and BC. More than $226.9 million has been reimbursed to the provinces since 2018.

## Chapter 1 – Canada Health Act overview
Canada's single-payor public health care insurance system, "Medicare", is financed through a progressive tax system, which allows risks to be pooled and costs to be shared by all Canadians. The health care system we have today developed over more than seven decades. In 1947, Saskatchewan was the first province to offer universal public hospital insurance to all residents. Ten years later in 1957, the federal government passed the Hospital Insurance and Diagnostic Services Act, which encouraged all provinces and territories to provide this coverage. By 1961, every province and territory offered public hospital insurance.
In 1962, Saskatchewan led the country again by introducing coverage for physician services. The federal government followed with the Medical Care Act in 1966. By 1972, physician services were covered in every province and territory across the country.
In 1979, a review of the health system was performed by Justice Emmett Hall at the request of the federal government. It found that health care in Canada was among the best in the world. However, it also warned that hospitals and physicians charging extra fees to patients were creating barriers to care. This review and the national debate that followed led to the Canada Health Act, which codified the national principles underpinning federal funding for hospital and physician services and added prohibitions on the patient charges which threatened to undermine universal access to care. The Canada Health Act passed unanimously in the House of Commons in 1984.
"…we, as a society, are aware that the trauma of illness, the pain of surgery, the slow decline to death, are burdens enough for the human being to bear without the added burden of medical or hospital bills penalizing the patient at the moment of vulnerability. The Canadian people determined that they should band together to pay medical bills and hospital bills when they were well and income earning. Health services were no longer to be bought off the shelf and paid for at the checkout stand. Nor was their price to be bargained for at the time they were sought. They were a fundamental need, like education, which Canadians could meet collectively and pay for through taxes."
Justice Emmett M. Hall, Canada's National-Provincial Health Program for the 1980's, 'A Commitment for Renewal', (Health services Review 1979-1980), Chapter 1, pg. 6, August 29, 1980

### What is the Canada Health Act?
In Canada, the roles and responsibilities for health are shared between the federal, provincial and territorial governments, as set out in the constitutional division of powers.
The provincial and territorial governments have primary jurisdiction in health care administration and delivery. This includes setting their own priorities, administering their health care budgets, and managing their own resources.
The federal government, under the Canada Health Act, sets the conditions that provinces and territories must meet in order to receive federal health funding, via the Canada Health Transfer. The Act aims to ensure that Canadians can access hospital and physician services, on a prepaid basis, without paying extra fees out-of-pocket.
Not all health services are covered under the Canada Health Act. Uninsured services include non-medically necessary care, such as preferred accommodation, private duty nursing, the provision of telephones and televisions, cosmetic procedures, and administrative services like medical certificates or record transfers.
Provinces and territories may also offer additional programs, in addition to the services they provide under the Canada Health Act, such as prescription drugs, dental care, ambulance services, and optometry. These are provided at provincial discretion, often targeting specific groups, with coverage and funding varying by jurisdiction.
In fiscal year 2024-2025, the federal government transferred $52.1 billion to provinces and territories through the Canada Health Transfer, providing a stable and flexible funding base that supports their health care systems. Additional information on federal, provincial and territorial funding arrangements is available by visiting the Department of Finance's website.
In addition to the Canada Health Transfer, Budget 2025 re-confirmed the federal government's plan, Working Together to Improve Health Care for Canadians, which includes $25 billion over 10 years to improve access to family health, mental health and substance use services, support the health workforce and modernize health systems, as well as $8.1 billion to improve access to home and community care, and safe long-term care.
Initial agreements with provinces and territories were in place by March 2024, anchored in fundamental principles, including a mutual commitment to upholding the Canada Health Act. The Working Together plan marked a significant investment in ensuring equitable access to health care services across Canada, fostering collaboration between federal and provincial and territorial authorities to address systemic challenges and enhance health care delivery. Initial signs of progress include growth in the number of health professionals, greater availability of integrated youth mental health services, and a higher number of surgeries completed than pre-pandemic levels. Building on the success of these initial agreements, work is underway with provinces and territories to provide access to the remaining Working Together investment.

### Key definitions under the Canada Health Act
Insured health services are medically necessary hospital, physician, and surgical-dental services (when they must be delivered in hospital to be performed safely).
Extended health care services include certain aspects of long-term residential care (like nursing home and adult residential care), as well as the health aspects of home care and ambulatory care services.
Insured persons refers to residents of a province or territory. This does not include tourists, transients or visitors to the province.
Certain groups are excluded from the Act's definition of "insured persons," including members of the Canadian Armed Forces and federal inmates, who are covered through separate federal programs. These exclusions predate the Act and do not limit access to publicly insured health care.
A health care practitioner is a person lawfully entitled under the law of a province to provide health services.

### Requirements of the Canada Health Act
In order to qualify for full Canada Health Transfer contributions, the health care insurance plans of provinces and territories must satisfy the nine requirements of the Canada Health Act, including:
- five program criteria that apply only to hospital and physician services (insured health services);
- two conditions that apply to insured health services and extended health care services; and
- two provisions regarding patient charges, that apply only to insured services.

### The Canada Health Act infographic
If the provinces and territories fulfil the Canada Health Act's 5 criteria and 2 conditions:
- Public administration
And ensure there are no patient charges for insured health services that constitute extra-billing or user charges they are entitled to their full Canada Health Transfer.
Public Administration (Section 8) requires provincial and territorial health care insurance plans to be publicly administered on a non-profit basis, accountable to their governments, and subject to public audits.
This criterion does not prohibit private delivery. Insured services may be provided by private facilities or practitioners, provided the public plan pays for them and patients are not charged.
Comprehensiveness (Section 9) requires that provincial and territorial health care insurance plans cover all insured hospital, physician, and surgical-dental services.
The Canada Health Act does not define medical necessity. This is because provinces and territories have primary jurisdiction in the administration and delivery of health care services, including determining which services to cover under their public health care insurance plans.
Insured services do not include those provided under another Act of Parliament. Examples include services provided to inmates of federal penitentiaries or members of the Canadian Armed Forces, as well as services covered by provincial or territorial workers' compensation legislation.
Universality (Section 10) entitles all insured residents of a province or territory to insured health services on uniform terms and conditions.
Eligibility for health insurance coverage is not based on citizenship, rather it is based on provincial and territorial residency. Provinces and territories generally require that residents register with the plan to establish entitlement.
Portability (Section 11) ensures health coverage continues when residents move within the country or travel in Canada or abroad. When moving to another province or territory, the home province plan covers insured services during the waiting period (up to three months) before the new plan begins. Residents must inform their home plan and register with the new plan to avoid gaps in coverage.
For temporary absences within Canada , the home plan pays at the host province's rate.
For absences outside Canada , the home plan pays at its own rate, and reimbursement must be requested.
The portability criterion is intended to allow a person to receive medically necessary services for urgent or emergent needs, when absent on a temporary basis (e.g., business or vacation). However, it does not entitle residents to seek elective services (non-urgent) or shorter wait times outside their home province or territory. Prior approval may be required for non-emergency care obtained outside the home province or territory.
See Chapter 2, Interprovincial Health Insurance Agreement Coordinating Committee, for details on how provinces and territories support portability within Canada.
Accessibility (Section 12) requires reasonable access to insured services, under uniform terms and conditions. Access must not be limited by patient charges (such as extra-billing or user charges) or by discrimination based on factors like age, race, health status, or financial circumstances.
Provincial and territorial health care insurance plans must also compensate physicians and dentists for insured services and pay hospitals for the cost of those services.
Information (Section 13A)
Provinces and territories must provide information to the federal Minister of Health as required by regulations under the Act.
Recognition (Section 13B)
Provinces and territories must acknowledge federal financial contributions to insured and extended health services.
Extra-Billing and User Charges (Sections 18–21) are collectively referred to as "patient charges" and are prohibited under the Act as they create barriers to care. If it can be confirmed that either extra-billing or user charges exist in a province or territory, the Act requires a mandatory dollar-for-dollar deduction from Canada Health Transfer payments to that province or territory.
Extra-Billing (Section 18) occurs when an enrolled physician (or an enrolled dentist providing insured surgical-dental services) charges an insured person more than the amount payable by the public plan for an insured service.
User Charges (Section 19) are fees for insured services other than extra-billing. Any fee required as a condition for receiving an insured service is a user charge. In other words, if patients were charged a fee as a condition of receiving insured health services, that fee would be considered a user charge.
What is a patient charge?
- If an enrolled medical practitioner or dentist …
- Charges an insured resident …
- For an insured health service …
- An amount in addition to the amount paid by the provincial or territorial health care insurance plan that…is extra-billing.
- Other charges (e.g., for supplies) related to the provision of insured health services …are user charges.

#### Other elements of the Act
Regulations (Section 22)
Section 22 allows the federal government to set regulations for administering the Act. These include:
- defining "extended health care services" (e.g., nursing or home care);
- specifying services excluded from hospital coverage;
- setting requirements for information submission by provinces and territories to the federal Minister of Health; and
- outlining how provinces and territories must recognize the Canada Health Transfer.
The only regulations in force are the Extra-billing and User Charges Information Regulations. These Regulations require annual provincial and territorial reporting on patient charges to Health Canada (see Annex C ).
Provinces or territories that allow patient charges face mandatory dollar-for-dollar deductions from Canada Health Transfer payments.
In practical terms, if patient charges occur, the Canada Health Transfer payment to the province or territory must be reduced by an amount equal to the patient charges (i.e., dollar for dollar).
Deductions are usually based on provincial or territorial reports as required by regulations. If information is missing or incomplete in these reports, Health Canada is required to estimate the amounts in consultation with the province or territory.

#### Discretionary Penalties
Non-compliance with any of the five criteria or two conditions of the Act can lead to discretionary penalties. The deduction amount depends on the level of non-compliance and must be approved by Cabinet. Consultation with the province or territory is required before any discretionary penalty is applied. To date, discretionary penalties have not been used.

### Keeping the Canada Health Act contemporary

#### Interpretation letters
When evolutions in the health care system have led to patient charges, federal Ministers of Health have issued clarifications of the Act's intent and application through interpretation letters to their provincial and territorial counterparts.
There have been four interpretation letters since the Act was enacted. Copies of the letters can be found in Annex B .
A year after the passage of the Act, this letter provided details on how the Act would be administered, and highlighted the fundamental change signified by the Canada Health Act, which was the prohibition of all patient charges for insured services. This letter remains an important reference for assessing and interpreting compliance with the Act.
The Federal Policy on Private Clinics clarified that patient charges for insured services at private clinics are subject to mandatory penalties under the Act. The letter stated that the definition of hospital contained in the Act includes any facility that provides acute, rehabilitative or chronic care.
The Diagnostic Services Policy clarified that patients should not face charges for medically necessary services, including diagnostic services, whether provided in a public health care facility or private clinic.
The Reimbursement Policy allows provinces and territories to be reimbursed for mandatory deductions when they eliminate patient charges for insured services and the circumstances that led to them within a specified timeframe.
The aim of strengthened Canada Health Act reporting is to ensure Health Canada has the information required to accurately assess compliance with the Act, as well as to increase transparency for Parliament and Canadians on the administration of the Act, and the state of the publicly funded health care insurance system.
The Canada Health Act Services Policy clarified that patients must not be charged for medically necessary services provided by regulated health professionals if the same services would be covered by provincial or territorial health care plans when performed by a physician. The policy ensures that the same basket of hospital and physician services insured under the Canada Health Act in 1984 remains insured as the health care system evolves.
The Services Policy takes effect April 1, 2026. Provinces and territories will report patient charges for physician-equivalent services beginning in December 2028.

### Canada Health Act mythBusters
Myth: All health care in Canada must be publicly delivered.
Fact: The Canada Health Act doesn't forbid the provision of health services by private companies, as long as residents are not charged for insured health services. In fact, many aspects of health care in Canada are delivered privately. Family physicians mostly bill the provincial or territorial health care insurance plan as private contractors. Hospitals are often incorporated private foundations, and many aspects of hospital care (e.g., lab services, housekeeping, and linens) are carried out privately. Lastly, in many provinces and territories, private facilities are contracted to provide services under the health care insurance plan.
Myth: Health care in Canada is free.
Fact: While you may not have to pay upfront when you receive medically necessary services, health care in Canada is not free. Health care in Canada is funded through tax revenues at the provincial, territorial, and federal levels. By spreading the cost of health care across the entire population, everyone is assured of the care they need, without the great financial burden that medical expenses could pose to a family or individual.
If you believe you have been subject to inappropriate patient charges for insured health services please contact your respective province or territory using the information contained in the Contact Information section of the report, or by contacting the Canada Health Act Division at medicare.spb.chad-dlcs.dgps.assurance.maladie@hc-sc.gc.ca .
Myth: I can use my health insurance card to find a shorter waitlist in another province or territory.
Fact: Your health insurance card does not entitle you to seek out shorter waitlists in other provinces or territories. Although you are covered for insured health services during temporary absences from your home province or territory, prior approval may be required before coverage can be used for non-emergency services in another province or territory.
Myth: I'm a Canadian so I am automatically entitled to health care coverage.
Fact: Having Canadian citizenship does not entitle you to health care coverage, rather you must be an eligible resident within a province or territory. Canadians have their part to play in establishing and maintaining their health care coverage. In all provinces and territories, you are required to register for coverage, and then maintain your eligibility by renewing your coverage, and remaining in your home province or territory for a prescribed number of days each year. Although allowance is often made if you leave your home province or territory for school, work, or other reasons, it is important to inform your provincial or territorial health care insurance plan when you will be away for extended periods, and to understand what your responsibilities are in maintaining your coverage.
Myth: My specific medical condition is covered under the Canada Health Act.
Fact: The Canada Health Act is quite a short piece of legislation and lays out standards at a very high level. Specific medical conditions are not named under the Act; rather, it requires provincial and territorial health care insurance plans to cover medically necessary hospital and physician services. Given their role in health care delivery, the decision over which services to cover is made by the province or territory, in consultation with the medical profession.
Myth: I don't need travel insurance within Canada because I'm covered under Medicare.
Fact: This is a very common misconception, and one that could be quite costly under certain circumstances. Medicare ensures that if you leave your province or territory for a few hours, days or weeks, you will still have coverage for emergency medical services. The same is true during moves to other provinces or territories. However, the hospital and physician services covered under the Canada Health Act are not the only services you might need while outside your usual province or territory. Some services that are not covered by the Act (e.g., prescription drug coverage or ambulance services) are highly subsidized for residents, but not for visitors, which is why you should ensure you have adequate insurance coverage whenever you travel or move within the country.

## Chapter 2 – Administration and compliance

### Canada Health Act compliance
The Canada Health Act Division monitors provincial and territorial health insurance plans and advises the Minister on possible non-compliance with the Act. Information is gathered from provincial and territorial government officials, publications, nongovernmental organizations, media, and correspondence directly from Canadians.
Division staff assess concerns on a case-by-case, gathering facts and working with provincial or territorial officials. Many issues are resolved quickly, often stemming from misunderstandings or miscommunication about health care coverage or portability.
If, after initial enquiries, a concern remains, the Division asks the jurisdiction to investigate and report back. It is only when a matter remains unresolved that is it brought to the federal Minister of Health.

### Deductions and reimbursements under the Act
For the most part, provincial and territorial health care insurance plans meet, and often exceed, the requirements of the Canada Health Act. However, some issues and concerns remain. The most prominent of these relate to accessibility issues, and specifically patient charges for medically necessary health and diagnostic services at private clinics.
Each year, provinces and territories submit financial statements to Health Canada detailing extra-billing and user charges (patient charges). These statements are used to determine whether deductions to Canada Health Transfer payments are required. Deductions are typically based on information provided by the province or territory; however, where information is missing or incomplete, Health Canada will use the best available information to calculate the amount of patient charges. This process includes consultation with the implicated province or territory. These financial statements (along with Reimbursement Action Plans) are also used to inform reimbursements to the provinces and territories.
Health Canada entered into an agreement with Statistics Canada to directly survey private clinics (Survey of Health Care Clinics in Canada) to support a better understanding of the landscape of private clinics, the services they offer, and where patients may be facing barriers to access. The first year of data from the survey was published in January 2025 for the 2022-2023 fiscal year. The first year of the survey focused on MRI, CT and ultrasound diagnostic clinics, and provinces and territories used this data to help inform their annual financial statements of patient charges to Health Canada for 2025.
In March 2025 a total of $62,204,582 in deductions and $51,901,885 in reimbursements were made to provinces and territories' Canada Health Transfer (CHT) payments.

#### Newfoundland and Labrador
In March 2025, $3,787 was deducted from Newfoundland and Labrador's CHT payments as a result of a single patient charge for cataract services. As the province has implemented regulatory and policy measures to strengthen prohibitions against these patient charges, as detailed in its Reimbursement Action Plan (RAP), the deduction was immediately reimbursed to the province.
In March 2025, $720,635 was deducted from Nova Scotia's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services. The province successfully implemented a RAP to eliminate the charges and the circumstances that led to them. They did so by contracting with the clinic in question to provide insured diagnostic services paid for by the health insurance plan, allowing for a reimbursement of March 2023, 2024, and 2025 deductions, totalling $3,792,929.
In March 2025, $850,835 was deducted from New Brunswick's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for insured abortion ($130,200) and medically necessary diagnostic ($720,635) services.
The province successfully implemented a RAP to eliminate patient charges related to abortion as well as the circumstances that led to them. The province repealed regulatory provisions which had limited coverage for abortion services to hospitals only. This issue is now considered resolved and CHT deductions from both March 2024 and 2025 were reimbursed to the province, totalling $239,475.
In March 2025, $35,249,525 was deducted from Quebec's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services. In recognition of ongoing efforts to improve access and eliminate patient charges for these services, an immediate partial reimbursement of $21,149,715 was also issued.
In March 2025, $26,240 was deducted from Ontario's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for insured abortion services. While the Ontario Health Insurance Plan provides coverage for physicians' fees related to abortion services in all settings, including private clinics, during the 2022-2023 reporting period the province only covered facility fees in abortion clinics licensed as Independent Health Facilities (IHF) under the Independent Health Facilities Act (IHFA). In some instances, this led to other clinics charging patients out-of-pocket to access abortion services. In September 2023, Ontario brought the Integrated Community Health Services Centre Act (ICHSCA) into force, replacing the previous legislation and creating a new framework for delivering insured health services in private clinics. The Act continues to ban facility fees for insured services and also prohibits clinics from denying access to insured service based on whether a patient chooses to pay for an uninsured service.
One of the five clinics previously charging patients has closed, and the remaining clinics have updated their websites to confirm surgical abortions are covered by the Ontario Health Insurance Plan. In recognition of these ongoing efforts to improve access and eliminate patient charges, a partial reimbursement of $29,520 was issued for 2024 and 2025 deductions.
In March 2025, $1,074,150 was deducted from Saskatchewan's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services. The patient charges arose under Saskatchewan's Patient Choice Medical Imaging Act, which permits private MRI and CT facilities to charge patients directly for medically necessary diagnostic imaging services, if the clinic provides a scan of similar complexity to a patient in the public queue.
In March 2025, $759,250 was deducted from Alberta's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services. In recognition of ongoing efforts to improve access and eliminate patient charges for these services, including increased public funding and capacity for medically necessary diagnostic services, a partial reimbursement of $5,704,137 was issued for March 2023, 2024, and 2025 deductions.
In March 2025, $23,520,110 was deducted from British Columbia's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for insured surgical ($6,407,114) and medically necessary diagnostic ($17,112,996) services.
British Columbia has made positive strides to implement components of its RAP to improve access and eliminate patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services, including the expansion of public diagnostic capacity by adding scanners, extending operating hours, and purchasing several private clinics. The province also directed private clinics to stop charging patients for insured MRI services, assumed operation of three private surgical clinics and continues contracting with most remaining private surgical clinics under agreements that prohibit patient charges. In recognition of these efforts, a partial reimbursement of $20,982,322 was issued for March 2023, 2024, and 2025 deductions.
In March 2025, $50 was deducted from Yukon's CHT payments as a result of one case of physician extra-billing.

### Additional compliance issues

#### Enrollment and membership fees at private primary care clinics
Private primary care clinics that charge annual membership fees remain a concern under the Canada Health Act. These clinics often offer both insured services and uninsured services (e.g., massage therapy, nutrition counselling). While fees are said to cover only uninsured services, some clinics make them mandatory for access to insured care.
Whenever Health Canada learns of such charges, it advises provinces and territories to ensure patients understand that insured services cannot depend on paying membership fees. This may involve investigations or audits by the jurisdiction, with findings shared with Health Canada to confirm improper charges are eliminated. Health Canada also encourages clear legislation protecting access to insured services and banning out-of-pocket costs for them.
In 2023, Health Canada contacted Alberta after reports indicated a clinic was charging for faster access to insured care. Alberta subsequently investigated all membership-based clinics in the province, identifying 41 offering both insured and uninsured services. A review of seven clinics suggested fees were only for uninsured care, but Alberta noted audits were needed to confirm compliance. The province is determining next steps, and Health Canada continues to monitor the issue.
Since 2023, British Columbia has sought injunctions against two membership clinics in non-compliance with provincial legislation, which mirrors Canada Health Act prohibitions on charges associated with insured health care services. After consultations, both revised their business models and the injunctions were withdrawn. British Columbia continues to monitor such clinics and regularly provides updates to Health Canada.
Physician services received by Quebec residents when out-of-province are not reimbursed at host province rates, which is a requirement of the portability criterion of the Canada Health Act.
For all jurisdictions, except Prince Edward Island and the three territories, the per diem rates for out-of-country hospital services appear lower than home province or territory rates, which is contrary to the requirement of the portability criterion of the Act. These concerns have been raised with the implicated provinces, and Health Canada continues to monitor the issue.

### History of deductions, reimbursements, and refunds under the Canada Health Act

#### Contemporary administration and compliance (2018-present)
The introduction of the Reimbursement Policy (2018) and Diagnostic Services Policy (2020) are important milestones in the contemporary administration of the Canada Health Act.
The Reimbursement Policy provides a positive incentive for provinces and territories to come into compliance and eliminate patient charges by allowing reimbursement of mandatory deductions when provinces and territories eliminate patient charges for insured services and the circumstances that led to them within two years of the deduction having been made. The policy reinforces the aim of the Act, which is not to levy penalties, but rather to ensure patients do not pay for insured services already paid for by their taxes.
The Diagnostic Services Policy confirms that medically necessary diagnostic services are considered insured services for the purposes of the Canada Health Act, whether provided in a hospital or a private clinic.
The following deductions and reimbursements occurred between 2018 and 2025, with annual breakdowns noted in Figure X (by province, east to west):
Since the Canada Health Act Reimbursement Policy came into effect in 2018, $226,912,389 has been reimbursed to provinces in recognition of their efforts to eliminate patient charges for insured health services.
Since 2019, a total of $83,454 has been deducted from Newfoundland and Labrador's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for cataract services. The province has implemented regulatory and policy measures to strengthen prohibitions against these patient charges, as detailed in its Reimbursement Action Plan, and to date all mandatory deductions have been reimbursed.
Since 2023, a total of $3,792,929 has been deducted for Nova Scotia's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services. Nova Scotia entered into a Reimbursement Action Plan in 2025 and the circumstances that led to these deductions have now been resolved, resulting in full reimbursements.
Since 2020, a total of $4,367,170 has been deducted from New Brunswick's CHT payments as a result of patient charges for insured abortion and medically necessary diagnostic services.
Health Canada was obligated to estimate deduction amounts for abortion services from 2020-2024 based on information from Clinic 554, where the charges took place, and data from the Canadian Institute for Health Information. In 2025, New Brunswick entered into a Reimbursement Action Plan which resolved the issues that led to the deductions, allowing for reimbursement of the abortion-related portion of the March 2024 and 2025 deductions. Given reporting under the Canada Health Act is retrospective, New Brunswick will be subject to deductions in March 2026 but will be eligible for immediate reimbursement if patient charges for access to abortion services do not reoccur.
New Brunswick also faced deductions for patient charges related to medically necessary diagnostic services from 2023-present.
In March 2019, a deduction of $8,256,024 was taken from Quebec's CHT, reflecting patient charges which had occurred prior to the corrective legislative action taken by Quebec to eliminate these charges and was immediately reimbursed. This reimbursement was the first made under the Reimbursement Policy.
Quebec has also been subject to deductions totalling $113,130,881 since March 2023 for patient charges related to medically necessary diagnostic services. In March 2024 and 2025, Quebec received partial reimbursements totaling $67,878,529 for these deductions. These reimbursements acknowledged progress made to address these patient charges, including new legislation reorganizing Quebec's health care system to improve access and eliminate patient charges for all insured services as well as contracting with clinics to provide insured services.
Since 2021, a total of $112,305 has been deducted from Ontario's CHT payments due to patient charges related to insured abortion services.
In March 2024 and 2025, Ontario received partial reimbursements totalling $72,816 for its March 2022-2025 deductions. This reimbursement was based on progress made under Ontario's Reimbursement Action Plan to improve access and eliminate these patient charges, which includes conducting clinic investigations and directing them to update their websites to state abortion services can be accessed at no cost.
Manitoba was subject to deductions from its CHT payments totalling $354,477 in March 2023 and 2024 due to patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services. In March 2024, Manitoba received a full reimbursement following the successful implementation of a Reimbursement Action Plan, which eliminated patient charges for the medically necessary ultrasound scans that had been taking place at a private clinic.
Since 2023, Saskatchewan has been subject to deductions totalling $2,901,110 from its CHT payments due to patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services. Health Canada was obligated to calculate deduction amounts using information provided by the province. To date, Saskatchewan has not qualified for reimbursement; however, Saskatchewan's March 2024 and 2025 deductions remain eligible for full reimbursement until March 2026 and March 2027, respectively.
Since 2023, Alberta has been subject to $34,990,577 in total deductions from its CHT payments due to patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services. In 2024, Alberta entered into a Reimbursement Action Plan to improve access and eliminate patient charges by increasing funding and capacity for medically necessary diagnostic services. As a result of progress made in implementing its action plan, Alberta has received partial reimbursements totaling $26,242,933.
Since 2018, a total of $141,398,363 has been deducted from British Columbia's CHT payments due to patient charges for medically necessary surgical services at private surgical and diagnostic clinics.
Following the 2018 deduction, British Columbia developed a Reimbursement Action Plan aimed at eliminating patient charges. As the province successfully implemented many of the plan's elements including the use of compliance agreements in its contracting process, which prohibit contracted clinics from levying patient charges for insured health services, taking over the operation of three private surgical clinics and three private diagnostic clinics, and working on an Order in Council that would bring into force existing legislative provisions to ban patient charges for medically necessary diagnostic services, it received a series of partial reimbursements, totalling $119,991,752.

### Post-implementation administration and compliance (1987–2018)
Following the Act's initial three-year transition period, during which refunds to provinces and territories for deductions were possible, penalties under the Act did not reoccur until fiscal year 1994–1995.

#### Federal policy on private clinics
To address the issue of patient charges for insured services at private clinics the Federal Policy on Private Clinics was issued in 1995. The policy clarified that the definition of "hospital" set out in the Canada Health Act includes more than just buildings with a big "H" on them. Hospitals include any facility that provides acute, rehabilitative, or chronic care. As such, a hospital also covers health care facilities, such as clinics.
This policy resulted in renewed CHT deductions during this period. Annual breakdowns are noted in Figure X (by province, east to west):
Between 1996 and 2014, Newfoundland and Labrador was subject to $383,779 in CHT deductions due to facility fees in a private abortion clinic, patient charges for an MRI scan in a hospital, and patient charges stemming from an opted-out dental surgeon.
Between 1996 and 2007, Nova Scotia was subject to $378,937 in deductions as a result of patient charges to cover facility fees at a private clinic in Halifax as well as improper billings of a physician. A one-time positive adjustment of $8,121 was made to Nova Scotia's March 2006 payment to reconcile amounts actually charged in 2003-2004, as the penalty had been based on provincial estimates.
Between 2017-2018, Quebec was subject to deductions totalling $19,814,458 as a result of accessory fees reported by the Quebec Auditor General. In light of corrective action the Quebec government had already taken to eliminate accessory fees in January 2017, these amounts were subsequently returned to Quebec. These refunds served as the inspiration for the Canada Health Act Reimbursement Policy.
Between 1995-1998, Manitoba was subject to deductions totaling $2,055,000 due to patient charges anticipated by the province at surgical and ophthalmology clinics. However, during 2001–2002, a monthly deduction (October 2001-March 2002) was levied on the basis of a financial statement provided by Manitoba. The statement showed that actual patient charges ($2,355,201) between 1997-1999 were greater than the original estimates ($2,055,000).
Between 1995-1996, Alberta was subject to $3,585,000 in deductions as a result of patient charges for facility fees at clinics providing surgical, ophthalmological and abortion services. On October 1, 1996, Alberta prohibited private surgical clinics from charging patients a facility fee for medically necessary services for which the physician fee was billed to the provincial health care insurance plan.
Between 1995-2018, British Columbia was subject to $19,660,001 in total deductions as a result of various compliance issues.
In the early 1990s, a dispute over compensation between the British Columbia Medical Association and the provincial government led some doctors to opt-out of the public health insurance plan and charge patients directly. Many of these doctors billed patients more than what the public plan would reimburse, which constituted extra-billing. As a result, deductions totalling $2,025,000 began in May 1994 and continued until extra-billing was banned in September 1995, when changes to British Columbia's Medicare Protection Act took effect.
Between 2003-2018, British Columbia was subject to $17,635,001 in total deductions as a result of patient charges at private surgical clinics. Deductions amounts were based on estimates by the federal Minister of Health until 2017 when a joint Health Canada-British Columbia audit project was concluded. Beginning in March 2018 deductions reflected private clinic audit results, patient complaints, and publicly available evidence of patient charges by enrolled physicians at the Cambie Surgery Centre.
Since the passage of the Act, from April 1984 to March 2025, deductions totaling $339,072,608 have been taken from transfer payments in respect of the extra-billing and user charges provisions of the Act. This amount excludes deductions totaling $244,732,000 that were made between 1984 and 1987, and subsequently refunded to the provinces when extra-billing and user charges were eliminated.
