In April 2022, the Province purchased View Royal Surgical Centre and Seafield Surgical Centre. In May 2023, Northern Health acquired the lease and equipment at the Prince George Surgery Centre.
For fiscal year 2023-2024, B.C. confirms that False Creek Healthcare Centre has entered contracts with two health authorities, valued at $5.46 million:
The total amount paid by health authorities in 2023-2024 to private clinics for acute care services was $20.94 million—0.13 percent of the $15.6 billion health authorities reported spending in acute care over the same period. Footnote 17

#### B.C. Surgical Renewal Strategy
On May 7, 2020, the Province announced its commitment to Surgical Renewal, which was a $250 million action plan launched in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. In addition to the initial investment, on September 9, 2020, the Province announced operating funding of up to $1.6 billion and capital funding of $150 million in 2020-2021 to respond to the health- care requirements related to the COVID-19 pandemic response. Of this amount, approximately $187.5 million was allocated to support enhanced surgical capacity, including additional nursing staff and operating room costs.
Outside of the Surgical Renewal investments, Budget 2022included $303 million in incremental base budget funding over the next three years to continue the strategy to reduce wait lists for surgery and diagnostic imaging. Footnote 18
In Year 4 of Surgical Renewal, (fiscal year 2023-2024), there were 365,825 Footnote 19 scheduled and unscheduled surgeries performed in B.C., amounting to a 4.3 percent increase over 2022-2023 (Year 3 of Surgical Renewal) and a 9 percent increase over the pre-pandemic years of 2019-2020.
The key to achieving the mandate above is maximizing surgical capacity. In B.C., as in other provinces, this includes private surgical centres. Contracting with these centres increases publicly funded access for patients whose surgical day procedures can be done safely and efficiently in the community, leaving our hospital capacity for more complex surgeries and procedures. Under contract arrangements with clinics in the province, 11,454 surgeries were performed in 2023-2024; compared to the following:
- 13,021 surgeries in 2022-2023.
- 16,777 surgeries in 2021-2022.
- 13,862 surgeries in 2020-2021.
- 12,336 surgeries in 2019-2020.
These decreases in surgical volumes in 2022-2023 and 2023-2024 relate to the ending of one contract and the purchase of three private surgical clinics by health authorities in B.C.
These contract additions should eliminate an approximate equivalent in private billing. With these contracts, the health authorities have moved from short-term to multi-year terms of service (while maintaining termination clauses should extra billing occur). The stability of these contract arrangements will keep former private capacity in the public system in a sustainable manner.
In addition to these steps, B.C. is committed to increasing public system capacity within the health authorities. This includes opening new operating rooms and extending the operating hours in our hospitals. In 2023-2024, operating rooms ran 21,516 Footnote 20 more hours compared to 2022-2023, 48,393 Footnote 21 more hours compared to 2021-2022, 65,734 Footnote 22 more hours compared to 2020-2021, Footnote 23 and 51,689 Footnote 24 more hours compared to 2019-2020. The purchase of the Prince George Surgery Centre resulted in NHA performing an additional 1,512 surgeries.
Through the purchase of private clinics and by negotiating contracts with private clinics to provide MSP-insured beneficiary services instead of extra billing for the same services,
B.C. continues to demonstrate its commitment to addressing patient extra billing, as defined under the CHA.

#### Gastrointestinal (GI) Endoscopy Contracting
In 2023-2024, two facilities were contracted by health authorities to perform GI endoscopy procedures:
- New Westminster Surgical Centre (FHA).
- GI Research Institute (VCHA).
Similar to surgical contracts with health authorities, all private GI surgical centres that hold contracts with health authorities are required to be accredited through the NHMSFAP with the CPSBC and must also be approved by the Commission to bill MSP for publicly funded surgeries.
In 2023-2024, a total of 5,761 colonoscopies and other GI endoscopy procedures were performed in private surgical centres at no cost to patients. In comparison, 10,101 procedures were performed at private centres in 2021-2022 and 7,672 in 2020-2021. The decrease in volumes in 2023-2024 is related to the purchase of the View Royal Surgical Centre by the Island Health Authority in 2022.

#### Medical Imaging Volumes
In 2023-2024, health authorities in B.C. conducted 322,043 MRI exams and 994,073 CT exams. This is 69,516 more MRI exams and 188,489 more CT exams compared to pre-pandemic volumes in fiscal year 2019-2020. Despite increased volumes year-over-year, the demand for imaging has increased the waitlist of patients for imaging. As of period 7, 2025-2026 there are an estimated 109,485 exams waiting for MRI and 100,072 exams waiting for CT.
B.C. currently operates 46 MRI units, 75 CT units, and four PET-CT units across the province. This is 8.1 MRI and 13.2 CT units per million population in 2024, which is below the Canadian 2023 averages of 10.8 MRI and 14.0 CT units per million population, and well below the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development averages of 19.4 MRI and 29.9 CT units per million population.
To support medical imaging in B.C., the Ministry established a Provincial Medical Imaging Office in the PHSA in early 2024. The key objectives of the provincial medical imaging office are to develop key metrics to monitor system improvements for access and wait times, work with regional health authorities on a capital investment priority framework, support the digitization of requisition management processes by regional health authorities, and develop a medical imaging roadmap for B.C. to improve access to publicly funded medical imaging services.
Significant investments are needed to address the shortage of imaging equipment as the requirement to replace aging equipment beyond their 15-year lifecycles continues to be considered. An added impact is the need to address shortages of Radiologists and Imaging Technologists for several imaging modalities (e.g., Medical Radiation Technologists, MRI Technologists, Sonographers, etc.) to effectively deliver publicly funded medical imaging services with shorter wait times.

#### Medical Imaging Contracts
Medical imaging services can be performed within the medical imaging department of a hospital or at a Community Imaging Clinic (CIC). To perform diagnostic imaging services in B.C., a medical imaging facility must be accredited for those specific imaging modalities by the Diagnostic Accreditation Program of the CPSBC.
Additionally, for certain imaging modalities, outpatient medical imaging clinics must be approved by the Advisory Committee on Diagnostic Facilities, an advisory committee to the Commission, to provide publicly insured services and bill the MSP for such services. In compliance with provincial legislation, a diagnostic facility approved by the Commission must not charge a beneficiary for a medically necessary insured benefit, such as an MRI; only health authorities or CICs contracted by a health authority can provide publicly funded MRI exams under the Hospital Insurance Act and its regulations. The purpose of the MPA legislation is to preserve a publicly managed and fiscally sustainable health care system in B.C. in which access to necessary medical care is based on need and not on an individual's ability to pay.
Since 2020-2021, VCHA and FHA have accessed exams on publicly owned MRI research units to deliver additional exams within their health authority's region. This has enabled the health authorities to complete more exams locally on underutilized research units that are publicly owned.
In 2021, VCHA purchased and converted a privately owned MRI clinic in Vancouver to a facility owned and operated by the health authority. In 2018-2019, FHA purchased two privately owned CICs, located in Surrey and in Abbotsford, with existing MRI units.
In 2023-2024, FHA initiated two separate contracts with privately owned CICs after a lengthy RFP process that require compliance with restrictions on extra billing. In 2024-2025, two CICs were contracted by FHA to provide publicly funded outpatient MRI and CT exams on behalf of the health authority. Integrations of data and image sharing between the private facilities and the health authority are ensuring there is no difference in the experience or quality for FHA's patients.
Additionally, BC Cancer has contracted with private PET-CT providers, in B.C. and in the United States, to serve cancer patients in a timely manner when waitlists are impacted or when equipment experiences unexpected downtime.

#### MRI at Community Imaging Clinics
In late 2023, the Commission advised three privately owned CICs operating MRI units and other imaging modalities that they would no longer be able to bill MSP for services provided to beneficiaries if they continued to operate private-pay MRI services for medically necessary MRI. Those clinics have all ceased providing privately paid MRI services to beneficiaries.
Private CICs form a vital part of the health system in B.C., and the majority comply with the Medicare Protection Act and Canada Health Act to maintain their good standing with the Commission and continue billing MSP for medically necessary imaging provided to beneficiaries.

#### Health Human Resources
B.C.'s Health Workforce and Health Human Resources Strategy
- In B.C., Canada, and around the world, health needs are becoming more complex and demand for health care services is growing.
- In response to increasing demand, B.C.'s health workforce continues to grow – thanks in part to key investments through B.C.'s Health Human Resources Strategy (the Strategy).
- In September 2022, B.C. began implementing the Strategy to optimize the health system, keep pace with the growing needs of people in B.C., and to ensure health services are accessible to everybody in B.C., now and in the future.
- The Strategy is focused on four priority areas or cornerstones: Training, Recruitment, Retention, and System Redesign. The goal of the Strategy is to strengthen the health care workforce with investment, expansion, and innovation in all four cornerstones across health professions, including physicians, nursing, and allied health professionals.
- Work across the Strategy is designed to drive Indigenous specific antiracism and move toward culturally safe and inclusive environments for professionals in the system.
- We are dedicated to making impactful change and meeting the health needs of all in British Columbia, and we are starting to see the impacts of our work.
- Since 2017 we have made progress to better support patients who rely on the public healthcare system—by adding more doctors, nurse practitioners, nurses, allied health professionals, health care assistants, and support staff.
- We know that people trained in B.C. stay in B.C., which is why we have added thousands of new health sector training seats across the province.
- We have launched successful international recruitment campaigns and improved credential recognition, training, and registration processes for internationally educated health-care workers.
- In addition to increasing training seats and expanding pathways for health workers to join the B.C. health care system, the Strategy emphasizes retention and focuses on sparking a new era of innovation that will strengthen team-based care and remove barriers to productivity.
- We know that our actions are working—for example, B.C. has an increasing supply of physicians compared to other provinces, and supply growth continues to outpace population growth.
Medically required laboratory services are publicly funded under the Laboratory Services Act (LSA). The Minister is responsible for all matters related to laboratory services, including facility approval, governance, accountability, and provision of benefits for all laboratory services in the province. The Agency for Pathology and Laboratory Medicine is now a program under the PHSA. The Agency's mandate is to provide laboratory system oversight and to ensure that clinical laboratory services are sustainable, quality driven, innovative, and support British Columbia's residents and clinicians with access to laboratory services. Since April 1, 2019, PHSA accepted accountability for operational functions assigned by the Ministry to support the LSA.

#### Ministry of Health Service Provider – Health Insurance BC
Pacific Blue Cross, doing business as PBC Solutions (PBCS), holds the contract to administer Health Insurance BC (HIBC) services on behalf of the Ministry. Under the contract, PBCS administers the MSP, PharmaCare, and laboratory programs, including responding to public inquiries, registering clients, and processing medical and pharmaceutical claims from health professionals. Policy and decision-making functions continue to remain with the Ministry.

#### Audits of Private Clinics
In 2017, Health Canada and the Ministry agreed upon a methodology to determine the extent and nature of patient extra billing in B.C., outlined in a Terms of Reference and Letter of Agreement. Since this agreement, the Ministry has completed 12 audits of private surgical clinics, including Cambie Surgeries Corporation. The results of these audits were shared with Health Canada in accordance with the signed agreement.
Since April 2022, for Health Canada reporting period 2020-2021, the Ministry is required to report extra billing attributed to private diagnostic facilities. A diagnostic audit framework has been developed, pending a decision on the implementation of section 18.1 of the MPA.
The purpose of these audits is two-fold:
- To monitor and assess compliance with the MPA.
- To help determine an accurate estimate of the extent of extra billing in the province.
The Ministry will provide the final audit reports for individual clinics and/or providers to Health Canada, subject to any redactions required to comply with the Freedom of Information and Personal Privacy Act. The Ministry is working through concerns and legalities around posting summarized versions of audit reports online.
Subject to clarification from the Court, the Ministry is committed to transparency and will continue to work with Health Canada in reviewing audit findings as work is completed.
Regular meetings with B.C. staff and Health Canada staff to discuss extra billing and other pertinent matters including audit findings remain ongoing.

#### Reporting Requirements
B.C. is submitting a complete and accurate 2023-2024 extra billing and user charges financial statement to Health Canada as per the reporting requirements set out in the CHA and Regulations. B.C. is reporting a total of $24,428,469 in extra billing and user charges that occurred in 2023-2024 according to the agreed-on reporting methodology.
In summary, B.C. continues to take all actions within its power to strengthen the public health care system, eliminate extra billing, and ensure full compliance with the CHA. The specific steps and strategies to reach this goal will evolve to address changing circumstances, but B.C.'s multi-pronged and robust approach, backed by significant resources, will stay constant.
B.C. will continue to focus on audit and enforcement and deterring extra billing, as well as ongoing efforts to educate both the public and practitioners in the province. We believe this status update demonstrates the robust commitment of our province in upholding the principles of the CHA under challenging circumstances.
By moving forward with the above noted actions, B.C. has taken the necessary steps to address extra billing within the province and is seeking reimbursement from Health Canada for the 2023-2024 penalty of $24,428,469.
It is our expectation that steps be taken under the authority of the federal Reimbursement Policy to reimburse B.C. for all extra billing penalties levied to date, including any balance of CHT funds eligible for reimbursement previously withheld by the Federal Government and any penalty that may arise from the filing related to practices in effect in 2023-2024.

#### Statement of Attestation
I attest that the above information captures the full extent of the Ministry of Health's efforts to eliminate extra billing and ensure full compliance with the CHA and its Regulations and applicable provincial/territorial legislation.
A/Assistant Deputy Minister and Executive Financial Officer Ministry of Health British Columbia
[Following is the text of Yukon's Reimbursement Action Plan]

### Yukon Reimbursement Action Plan
In 2022-2023 the Yukon reported a $50.00 in extra-billing on our Financial Statement of Actual Amounts of Extra-billing and User Charges. There was a Yukon physician who levied additional charges on a patient. The physician, who has not opted out of the Yukon Health Care Insurance Plan (the Plan), billed the Plan for insured services and then charged an additional amount for the same insured service on the rationale that those additional amounts were to "cover the cost of their equipment" because they are of the view that the Plan does not pay enough to cover their costs.

#### Investigation and reporting
A patient complaint about the extra-billing charges was made to the Manager of Registration & Claims. This triggered a reactive investigation about patient charges levied for chalazion excision in addition to the charges to the Plan.
The Director of the Plan met with the physician to discuss the patient charge and to gain an understanding of why they were billed in addition to those billed to the Plan.

#### Corrective taken and monitoring compliance
The physician was advised to reach out to the Yukon Medical Association about the negotiation fee code and its value for the services provided and request an adjustment to the fee code value that would then be tabled at a future Feel Liaison Committee for review and consideration. The physician was also advised to stop the practice of extra-billing.
No further patient charges have been observed.
